title: Hebrews: Better Revelation
objective: See Jesus as God's final and superior revelation, greater than angels and previous mediators.

required_passages.text: Long ago... God spoke... by the prophets, but in these last days he has spoken to us by his Son... we have a great high priest... let us hold fast our confession.

segments.audio_script: Hebrews opens by exalting Jesus above all previous revelation.

Listen to Hebrews 1-4. God has spoken finally in His Son, who is radiance of His glory. Jesus is greater than angels and worthy of worship. The writer warns against drifting and calls believers to enter God's rest by faith. Jesus is presented as the sympathetic high priest who invites us to approach the throne of grace confidently.
Jewish-background believers faced pressure to retreat from Christ to older patterns.
Hebrews argues not against the Old Testament but for its fulfillment in Christ. Perseverance begins by seeing Jesus clearly as supreme and sufficient.
Key themes include Christ's supremacy, final revelation, warning against drift, rest by faith, and priestly access to grace.

Review question: What does Hebrews 1:1-2 say about how God has spoken in these last days? Answer: in these last days he has spoken to us by his Son, whom he appointed heir of all things and through whom he made the universe.
segments.correct_answer: in these last days God spoke through his Son — heir of all things, through whom he made the universe
acceptable_variants: god spoke in the prophets but in these last days by his son who is heir and creator
hebrews 1 — the son is God's final and superior word surpassing the prophets
the son is the last word — appointed heir and agent of creation

segments.audio_script: Jesus is God's final word and our faithful high priest. Next, Hebrews shows His better priesthood and covenant.

vocabulary.definition: The key word in Hebrews appearing thirteen times; Christ is better than angels, Moses, Aaron, and the old covenant — establishing the complete superiority of the new covenant
The mysterious priest-king of Genesis 14 whose order of priesthood Hebrews applies to Christ in chapters 5-7; unlike Aaronic priests, Jesus holds a permanent priesthood after Melchizedek's order
